coredata.dc:title: Renewable and hydrophilic carbon quantum dots derived from human hair as the filler in Nafion composite membrane for vanadium redox flow battery application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Jeong, Dasom
Kim, Seong Cheon
Kang, Min Gu
Lim, Sung Nam
Woo, Ju Young
Hwang, Haejin
Choi, Siyoung Q.
Park, Jeasung
coredata.dc:description: Renewable and environmentally benign hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, composite membranes of Nafion/CQDs, demonstrating outstanding performance metrics for vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFBs), were developed. Employing a straightforward solution-casting technique, these membranes exhibited superior proton conductivity and reduced vanadium ion permeability. Tested at an elevated current density of 120 mA/cm2, the single cell VRFB integrated with the optimized Nafion/CQDs composite membrane outperformed its commercial counterparts, achieving a high coulombic efficiency of approximately 96.13 % and an energy efficiency of nearly 85.46 %. Moreover, it demonstrated significant cycle life, retaining about 17.1 % of its capacity after 100 cycles, compared to 27.2 % capacity retention for the N-recast membrane over the same period. These results position the Nafion/CQDs composite membrane as a formidable contender in the VRFB domain, emphasizing its potential for broader commercial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrophilic carbon quantum dots
Vanadium redox flow battery
Composite membranes
Capacity retention
Human hair
Biomass

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs at 50 nm magnification (a), and at 5 nm magnification (b), the corresponding particle size distribution histogram (c), and XRD patterns of CQDs (d).
XPS spectra of CQDs (a) Survey spectrum, (b) C1s spectrum, (c) N1s spectrum, (d) O1s spectrum, and (e) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs.

Quantum characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). (a) UV–vis analysis, and (insert in Fig. 3.a) Emission characteristics of CQDs as a function of wavelength, (b) photoluminescence (PL) characteristics and (insert in Fig. 2.b) normalized PL intensity.
FT-IR spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) N-recast membrane, (C) N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane.

(a) The water uptake values and swelling ratios of the membranes, (b) the permeability values and the proton conductivities of the membranes, and (c) the VO2+ concentration changes in the left reservoir of the cell with the membranes.
The Nyquist plots of different membranes.

The charge-discharge curves of the single cell with different membranes at 100 mAcm−2 in 1st cycle (a) and 100th cycle (b), OCV decay curves based on the membranes at SOC of 100 % (b), and the cell efficiency with N-recast and N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane under various current densities from 40 to 120 mAcm−2 (c).
The efficiencies and discharge capacity retentions of the single cells with the membranes under 100 mAcm−2.
Summaries of cycle life and capacity decay rate of modified Nafion membranes for vanadium redox flow batteries.
Schematic synthesis for the generation of CQDs derived from human hair
Elemental analysis of the CQDs derived from human hair.

head.ce:title: Renewable and hydrophilic carbon quantum dots derived from human hair as the filler in Nafion composite membrane for vanadium redox flow battery application

ce:note-para.ce:italic: These two authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Renewable and environmentally benign hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Subsequently, composite membranes of Nafion/CQDs, demonstrating outstanding performance metrics for vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFBs), were developed. Employing a straightforward solution-casting technique, these membranes exhibited superior proton conductivity and reduced vanadium ion permeability. Tested at an elevated current density of 120 mA/cm2, the single cell VRFB integrated with the optimized Nafion/CQDs composite membrane outperformed its commercial counterparts, achieving a high coulombic efficiency of approximately 96.13 % and an energy efficiency of nearly 85.46 %. Moreover, it demonstrated significant cycle life, retaining about 17.1 % of its capacity after 100 cycles, compared to 27.2 % capacity retention for the N-recast membrane over the same period. These results position the Nafion/CQDs composite membrane as a formidable contender in the VRFB domain, emphasizing its potential for broader commercial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As global energy consumption increases, there is a heightened focus on renewable energy sources like hydropower and wind power, driven by the scarcity of fossil fuels and escalating environmental issues [1,2]. The intermittent and less reliable nature of renewable energy compared to conventional sources necessitates its integration with energy storage systems (ESS) to support large-scale applications [3–5]. Therefore, developing reliable energy storage technologies is essential for harnessing innovative energy solutions [6].

Vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) stand out with their long lifespan, adaptable design, and quick response, marking them as one of the leading energy storage technologies [7,8]. In VRFBs, the membrane separator is crucial as it isolates the anolyte and catholyte, facilitating ion charge carrier movement and maintaining cell electrochemical neutrality [9–11]. The reversible reactions at the VRFB electrodes, driven by vanadium redox couples, are depicted in the equations [12,13].

The separator membrane significantly impacts VRFB performance. It influences the cell stack's efficiency, cycle life, and cost [14]. An ideal membrane should exhibit elevated levels of proton conductivity, negligible vanadium permeability, outstanding chemical and mechanical durability, and a cost within reasonable limits [15,16]. Perfluorosulfonic polymers, such as Nafion (manufactured by Dupont, USA), are extensively employed in VRFB systems owing to their exceptional proton conductivity and remarkable chemical and mechanical durability [17,18]. Nevertheless, a fundamental drawback of these polymers is the discrepancy between the size of their proton conduction channels, which typically range from 1.5 to 5 nm, and the dimensions of the hydrated multivalent vanadium ions, which measure approximately 0.6 nm. This mismatch results in a significant vanadium ion permeability, leading to detrimental consequences such as pronounced self-discharge and instability in the thermodynamic cycle [19–21].

An efficacious approach to the pursuit of a suitable separator membrane for VRFB systems entails the construction of a composite membrane. Significant endeavors have been dedicated to identifying composite membrane materials (fillers) with superior performance, employing straightforward and ecologically conscious modification/manufacturing techniques [22]. Previous research has demonstrated that incorporating hydrophilic organic components, inorganic materials, or a combination of both into a polymer matrix has resulted in enhancements in corrosion resistance, chemical and thermal stability, and proton conductivity [23]. In their study, Ye et al. [24] utilized lignin as the filler material, characterized by its plentiful hydrophilic groups and remarkable dispersibility. The inclusion of lignin in the Nafion composite membrane increased proton conduction pathways due to its hydrophilic properties. This enhancement significantly facilitated protons' transportation while simultaneously reducing vanadium ions' permeability. In their study, Long et al. [25] observed a notable enhancement in the proton conductivity of the composite membrane with the incorporation of s-MC containing –SO3H functional groups. In a study conducted by Choi et al. [26], it was observed that the performance of the VRFB system was improved by incorporating a Nafion composite membrane with two hydrophilic groups. This enhancement was achieved by introducing a metal-organic framework (MOF) containing -OH, -CH3, -OS1, and -OS2 groups into the Nafion/CAU-10-X (X = -OH, -CH3, -OS1, -OS2) composite membrane. These previous research had indicated that the hydrophilic properties of filler materials used in VRFB composite membranes play a pivotal role in enhancing overall membrane performance, primarily by increasing proton conductivity and decreasing vanadium ion permeation. Nonetheless, the field application of these composite Nafion membranes is constrained due to the relatively high cost of the filler materials. Consequently, the ongoing academic challenge remains to identify and explore filler materials that can simultaneously satisfy the improved performance, increased durability, and economic viability of Nafion composite membranes.

Among a diverse range of potential filler materials, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) extracted from human hair are distinguished by their numerous hydrophilic groups and excellent dispersibility. These CQDs are not only environmentally friendly but also economically advantageous. Despite these benefits, research on hair-derived CQDs is still emerging. Hou et al. [27] and Liu et al. [28] have successfully synthesized CQDs from human hair without using acids or bases, confirming the high presence of hydrophilic groups in these CQDs. Based on these findings, our research proposed that incorporating CQDs as a filler in Nafion composite membranes not only enhances proton conductivity but also strategically improves the performance of VRFBs, offering significant environmental and economic benefits.

In this study, we developed a novel type of hydrophilic Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) derived from human hair, employing an environmentally friendly synthesis method. These CQDs exhibit nanoscale luminescent properties. Using the solution casting method, we fabricated a Nafion/CQDs composite membrane. This membrane incorporates hydroxyl groups from CQDs into its matrix, significantly enhancing proton conductivity and promoting efficient proton transport. Additionally, this configuration reduced the permeability of vanadium ions, thereby improving the performance and stability of the composite membrane. We conducted comprehensive investigations and analyses of the physicochemical properties of the Nafion-CQDs composite membrane and its performance metrics in VRFBs. The efficacy of this composite membrane in VRFBs was validated through evaluations of the device's lifespan, efficiency, rate capability, and self-discharge performance. Our findings demonstrate that CQDs are not only environmentally friendly but also improve the efficiency and stability of Nafion composite membranes, confirming their potential for broad application in energy storage technologies.

The chemicals in this research were commercially available and without further purification. N,N-dimethylformamide (>99 %) (DMF), sulfuric acid (98.0 %), Magnesium sulfate (98.0 %), and acetone (99.5 %) was purchased from Samchum Chemicals. A Nafion perfluorinated resin solution (5 wt% in lower aliphatic alcohols and water) was purchased from DuPont. Malaysian virgin straight hair was purchased online on Amazon.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method, as outlined in earlier studies [27]. Initially, 1 g of human hair was cleaned ultrasonically with deionized (DI) water and acetone, then air-dried. The dried hair was then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave filled with 40 mL of DI water and heated to 200 °C for six hours. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The mixture was then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove larger particles. The clear supernatant was filtered first through a 450 nm pore-size filter and then three times through a 220 nm pore-size filter, resulting in a light-brown solution enriched with CQDs. For preservation, the solution was lyophilized to obtain a pale-yellow crystalline powder of CQDs.

Nafion-CQDs composite membranes were fabricated using the solution casting method, as previously described in the literature [26]. We synthesized membranes with three different concentrations of CQDs, designated as N-CQDs (0.x), where x represents concentrations of 8, 12, and 16, to study the effect of CQD concentration on membrane performance.

The fabrication process began by evaporating a Nafion perfluorinated resin solution using a rotary evaporator to isolate the Nafion resin. This resin was then dispersed in dimethylformamide (DMF) using ultrasonic methods to achieve a 10 % weight solution of Nafion in DMF. Concurrently, the measured CQD powder was added to the Nafion-DMF solution. To prevent CQD aggregation, the mixture was subjected to ultrasonic dispersion for one hour, followed by magnetic stirring for 12 h to ensure CQDs were evenly distributed throughout the solution.

Finally, the Nafion-DMF and CQD blend was cast onto a glass substrate using a specific casting apparatus. The substrate was then heated to 80 °C for four hours on a heating plate to facilitate solvent evaporation. The process was completed by polymerizing the residue in a vacuum oven at 140 °C overnight.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Shimadzu LabX XRD-6100. High-resolution imaging and nanostructure determination were carried out with a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin transmission electron microscope (TEM). Surface and elemental compositions were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted with a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer, covering a wavenumber range from 4000 to 650 cm−1, also provided by Thermo Fisher Scientific. For detailed surface morphology, field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was executed with a Jeol JSM-6701F. Optical properties were investigated using two methods: UV–vis absorption spectra from 200 to 600 nm were measured with a PerkinElmer Lambda 750C, and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were assessed at intervals of 20 nm between excitation wavelengths of 300 to 360 nm using a HAMATSU UV-VIR ABSOLUTE PL QUANTUM YIELD spectrometer C13534.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptake, swelling ratio, proton conductivity, and VO2+ permeability
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the water uptake (WU) and swelling ratio (SR) of the membranes, we cut the specimens into congruent rectangular sections and dried them in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 24 h. After drying, we recorded the weights and lengths of the specimens with a minimum of three measurements per sample. The specimens were then immersed in deionized water for another 24 h. Following immersion, we removed residual moisture by gently dabbing the samples with an absorbent medium before immediately measuring them. We calculated WU and SR using the following formulas, detailed in reference [29]:
WU =
W wet
−
W dry
W dry
× 100 % .
SR =
L wet
−
L dry
L dry
× 100 %
wherein
W wet
and
W dry
represent the wet and dry sample weights, respectively, while
L wet
and
L dry
correspond to their respective lengths.

The proton conductivity of the synthesized membranes was derived from their membrane resistance, measured via impedance spectroscopy as outlined in references [23, 26]. Initially, the membranes were soaked in a 1 M H2SO4 solution for 24 h, then resized to fit a custom-manufactured stainless steel cell equipped with Cu wire for impedance measurements. We recorded the AC impedance using an Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscope (CHI6016E, CH Instruments) at 298 K, across a frequency range from 1 Hz to 0.1 MHz. Proton conductivity was calculated using the following formula:
Proton conductivity
mS / cm
=
d
R 1
−
R 2
· S
Where d and S signify the membrane's thickness (μm) and area (cm2) of the membranes, R1 stands for the membrane-incorporated cell resistance (Ω), and R2 represents the standalone cell resistance (Ω).

VO2+ permeability was measured following methodologies detailed in previous research [30]. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the specially crafted membrane was positioned between the orifices of custom-designed glass receptacles. One receptacle was filled with a 200 mL solution of 1.6 M MgSO4 in 2.0 M H2SO4, and the other was filled with an equivalent volume of 1.6 M VOSO4 in 2.0 M H2SO4. To control for osmotaxis, magnetic stirrers operating at 200 rpm ensured consistent mixing within both receptacles. During each sampling event, 1 mL was sampled from the right receptacle and an identical volume was simultaneously discarded from the left to maintain osmotic balance. The absorption spectrum of the extracted samples, ranging from 500 to 800 nm, was analyzed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600 Plus, SHIMADZU) to determine the concentration of permeated VO2+. Based on this concentration, ion permeability was calculated using the provided mathematical model.
V R
d
c R
t
dt
= A
P L
C L
−
C R
t

Where P symbolizes the ion permeability, represented in cm2 min−1, A and L respectively denote the membrane's surface area (cm2) and diameter (μm) of the membrane, the concentrations of MgSO4 and VOSO4 are captured by CL and CR respectively (M), V is the volume of the solution (mL), and t is time (min).

The ion selectivity was subsequently determined by calculating the ratio of the previously computed proton conductivity to the permeability of VO2+ and expressing it as a percentage (proton conductivity/VO2+ permeability × 100 (%)).

A VRFB single cell (Fig. S1d) was meticulously assembled using carbon electrodes measuring 33 mm × 33 mm. Supplementary components, including the PTTE flow frame, graphite bipolar plate, current collector, and an end frame, were standardized to dimensions of 115 mm × 115 mm. This assembly was symmetrically aligned with respect to the ion exchange membrane, which measures 40 mm × 40 mm. The chosen electrolyte solution was composed of 50 mL of a 1.6 M solution of V 3+ and V4+, dissolved in 2.0 M H2SO4. To evaluate performance dynamics during charge and discharge cycles, a sophisticated battery testing apparatus, the WBCS3000 (by WONATECH), was used. Charging thresholds were set with a maximum voltage of 1.65 V, while discharge parameters were set with a minimum voltage of 0.8 V to prevent potential oxidation of the carbon electrodes. Subsequent calculations for metrics such as Coulomb efficiency (CE), voltage efficiency (VE), and energy efficiency (EE) were derived using the following formulas:
CE
%
=
discharge capacity charge capacity
× 100
%
VE
%
=
middle point of discharge voltage middle point of charge voltage
× 100
%
EE
%
= CE × VE

Hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs), exhibiting pronounced blue fluorescence, were proficiently synthesized as shown in Scheme 1 . Notably, human hair was used as the primary carbon source for this synthesis. The process was carried out through a streamlined one-step hydrothermal method at 200 °C for a duration of six hours. Importantly, this synthesis did not involve any catalysts and required no subsequent surface passivation steps, highlighting its simplicity and environmental friendliness.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images of the CQDs, delineated in Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b, confirm their derivation from human hair and reveal a quasi-spherical configuration. The images show a consistent dispersion pattern with no evident aggregation, and particle diameters ranging from 1 to 14 nm. A histogram illustrating this size distribution is presented in Fig. 1c, indicating that the majority of particles measure between 3 and 8 nm. Statistical analysis calculated the average diameter to be approximately 6.67 nm. Further analysis into the crystalline characteristics of the CQDs was performed through X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Fig. 1d. The XRD patterns are emblematic of amorphous carbon, a finding corroborated by prior studies [31]. A pronounced, broad peak is discernible at around 20.46° (or 4.43 Å), which aligns with the [002] facet typical of sp2-bonded graphite. It is pertinent to note that the typical interlayer spacing of graphite, usually measured at 3.3 Å, appears slightly increased in these CQDs, at around 4.43 Å. This discrepancy is likely due to the abundance of hydrophilic functional groups interspersed throughout the CQD structure.

Keratin, the primary component of human hair, is rich in elements such as carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The elemental composition of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from human hair was analyzed and is detailed in Table 1 . These CQDs have a carbon content of 43.98 %, highlighting their carbon-rich nature. Compared to the elemental composition of hair, there was a slight decrease in carbon content in the CQDs, but an increase in hydrogen and oxygen was observed. This suggests an enrichment of hydroxyl groups in the CQDs, potentially enhancing their functionality.

An exploration into the chemical composition and structural attributes of the synthesized CQDs was carried out. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2a identified carbon (C 1 s, 284.5 eV), nitrogen (N 1 s, 399.9 eV), and oxygen (O 1 s, 531.1 eV) components, aligning well with the findings from the elemental assessment. A closer look at the C 1 s peak (Fig. 2b) revealed the presence of distinct functional groups: CC (284.1 eV), CN (284.7 eV), CO (285.7 eV), and both CO and CN (287.6 eV) [32]. Meanwhile, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2.c) brought to light three nitrogen species: C-N-C (399.1 eV), NC3 (400.33 eV), and NH (401.0 eV) [32,33]. Furthermore, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) highlighted three key oxygen states: CO (530.3 eV), C-OH/C-O-C (531.0 eV), and O=C-O (532.2 eV) [32]. Evidently, the CQDs bore a multitude of functional groups, including hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid, giving them notable water solubility and a Zeta potential value of −20.5 mV [27]. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was used to delve deeper into the CQDs' chemical and structural properties (Fig. 2e). Notable absorptions included an OH band around 3392 cm−1 and a CO stretching vibration at 1671 cm−1, signifying the presence of oxygen-rich functionalities like carboxylic acid [34,35]. A broad peak near 3392 cm−1 and a more defined one at 1633 cm−1 corresponded to the NH group, indicating amino functionality. Three distinct peaks at 2963, 1404, and 1301 cm−1 were tied to CH, CC, and CC vibrations, respectively, hinting at the existence of both alkyl and aromatic components [32]. This FT-IR detection of abundant hydrophilic entities dovetailed seamlessly with the earlier XPS findings. Through these results, we successfully confirmed the effective synthesis of hydrophilic CQDs.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), particularly those with a particle diameter of less than 10 nm, are renowned for their luminescent properties, which arise from the quantum confinement effect. To validate the luminescence characteristics of the synthesized CQDs, both UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies were utilized. The UV–vis spectroscopy analysis of CQDs (see Fig. 3a) identified two significant peaks. The first, at 270 nm, can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC bonds, and the second, at 330 nm, is associated with the n-π* transition of CC bonds [7]. Moreover, when illuminated under a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm, the CQDs in aqueous dispersion exhibited a distinct blue fluorescence (Fig. 3a). This fluorescence is characteristic of CQDs within the 4–8 nm size range, attributed to their larger bandgap energies. Functional groups either within the π-π* orbital of the CC bonds or on the surface of the CQDs are thought to significantly influence this luminescent emission, though the exact mechanisms continue to be explored. The PL spectrum of the CQDs is shown in Fig. 3b. Systematic measurements of the emission wavelength, conducted with excitation wavelengths starting from 300 nm and increasing in 20 nm increments, demonstrated a rightward shift in the emission wavelength corresponding to increases in excitation wavelength. This distinctive shift in the PL traits of CQDs confirms the dependence of emission wavelength and intensity on the excitation wavelength [8]. These findings conclusively establish that the synthesized material possesses the unique luminescent properties characteristic of CQDs, confirming their identity and potential for various applications.

The chemical composition of the reformulated Nafion composite membrane (N-CQDs 1.2) was rigorously validated using FT-IR spectroscopy, comparing both the CQDs and the reformulated membrane against established benchmarks (refer to Fig. 2). In the spectrum of the Nafion re-cast membrane, absorption bands at 1148 cm−1 and 1204 cm−1 are observed, indicative of the antisymmetric vibration and symmetric stretch of CF2 groups, respectively. Additionally, the band at 1058 cm−1 corresponds to the SO vibration found in the – SO3 − side chain, a signature structural element of Nafion [23,24,36]. Peaks at 2963 cm−1, 1404 cm−1, and 1301 cm−1 suggest the presence of alkyl and aryl groups. Furthermore, the bands at 3392 cm−1 and 1671 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acids and other oxygen-rich functional groups [26]. The FT-IR spectrum of the N-CQDs 1.2 demonstrates distinct bands that are characteristic of both Nafion and CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 4 . Notably, the emergence of OH peaks around 3392 cm−1 and CO peaks at 1671 cm−1 in N-CQDs 1.2 confirms the inclusion of carboxylic acid groups and related oxygen-containing functionalities. These spectral features strongly suggest the successful integration of CQDs into the Nafion matrix, illustrating a harmonious blend of the original Nafion properties with the novel attributes introduced by the CQDs. This integration likely imparts enhanced functionalities to the composite membrane, underscored by the distinct FT-IR absorption characteristics.

The visual characteristics of N-CQDs solutions and their corresponding composite membranes are illustrated in Fig. S1. Remarkably, all these membranes are transparent. Both the Nafion/CQDs solutions and the composite membranes exhibit a deep brown color, which aligns closely with the inherent coloration of the CQDs. Notably, the intensity of the brown hue increases as the concentration of CQDs in the membranes rises, suggesting a seamless integration of CQDs with Nafion using the selected fabrication method. This results in composite membranes with consistent structural properties.

Furthermore, an in-depth evaluation of the operational efficiency of these composite membranes was conducted, focusing on key performance indicators such as water uptake (WU), swelling ratio (SR), VO2+ permeability, proton conduction pathways, and proton conductivity. The thickness of the reformulated Nafion membrane (N-recast) and the Nafion-CQDs composite membrane was made to a consistent size. This aspect could have significant implications for their functionality and application in various environments.

As depicted in Fig. 5a, the reconstituted membranes (N-recast) demonstrated minimal water uptake (WU), recorded at approximately 0.85 %. Notably, with the increase in CQD content, the WU also increased, a change likely attributable to the hydrophilic properties of the CQDs. It is well-recognized that hydrated ion transport plays a crucial role in proton conveyance [23,24], suggesting that the heightened WU in the composite membranes may enhance proton conductivity. The integration of CQDs led to a slight expansion compared to the N-recast membrane, indicating that the swelling ratio (SR) increased from 1.45 % to 2.32 % with the addition of up to 0.8 wt% CQDs. Beyond this point, the SR stabilized, possibly due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CQDs and Nafion [24]. Such dimensional stability potentially improves the durability of VRFB cycles. Further examination of ion transport within the membranes revealed significant insights regarding CQD permeability, as shown in Fig. 5b. A clear pattern emerged: VO2+ permeation slowed as CQD content increased. The permeabilities for N-CQDs 0.8 (8.03 × 10−7 cm2 min−1), N-CQDs 1.2 (6.52 × 10−7 cm2 min−1), and N-CQDs 1.6 (5.92 × 10−7 cm2 min−1) were notably lower than that of N-recast (14.84 × 10−7 cm2 min−1). Given the direct correlation between permeability and VO2+ concentration, it is plausible that CQDs obstruct VO2+ crossover, as reflected in the permeability trends observed. However, proton conductivity significantly decreased in N-CQDs 1.6. This result is attributed to the aggregation of CQDs. The details are discussed in Supplementary information (Discussion section 1.1 and Fig. S2). Furthermore, an investigation into ion transport, accentuated by the VO2+ concentration variations for each membrane type shown in Fig. 5c, revealed that the reconstituted Nafion membrane exhibited alarmingly high VO2+ permeability. The concentration of crossed VO2+ decreased significantly with increasing CQD content, particularly when compared to the N-recast values. The gradient, representing the relationship between VO2+ concentration and time, was less steep in the N-CQDs composite membranes, indicating enhanced ion selectivity (Table S1). This reduction in VO2+ concentration is likely due to the interference from CQDs, potentially through electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged functionalities of CQDs and VO2+ ions.

Fig. 6 displays the impedance spectra for various membranes, illustrating how membrane resistance can be quantified by the difference in intercepts on the real axis for cells with and without membranes. The measured resistances are as follows: N-recast at 0.4129, N-CQDs 0.8 at 0.3972, N-CQDs 1.2 at 0.3889, and N-CQDs 1.6 at 0.4367. Detailed data on area resistance can be found in Table 2 . Additionally, increasing CQD content correlates with enhanced conductivity, as observed in the rising values in Fig. 3a and the consistent trends in area resistance from Table 2. The reformulated membranes exhibit conductivity ranging from 10^-2 to 10^-3 S/cm, peaking near 22 mS/cm. Examining the ion transport mechanisms within these membranes, we see that both vehicle transport and Grotthuss mechanisms influence proton conduction, while active entities like vanadium ions also contribute to overall conductivity. This relationship between conductivity and permeability is crucial for assessing a membrane's ion selectivity, noted in reference [36]. Embedding CQDs into the Nafion matrix enhances proton channels through the addition of hydroxyl groups and reduces vanadium ion permeation, optimizing the membrane's functionality. An optimal CQD ratio of approximately 1.2 % is recommended to achieve the best performance, balancing enhanced conductivity and ion selectivity (Table S1).

Fig. 7a and b presents the charge-discharge curves for single cells using different membrane formulations: N-recast, N-CQDs 0.8, N-CQDs 1.2, and N-CQDs 1.6 (detailed in 2.4.3 and Fig. S1d). The tests were carried out 100 cycles at a current density of 100 mA cm−2 with voltage ranges between 0.8 V and 1.65 V. The charge-discharge test results for each composite membrane are displayed in the supplementary information (Fig. S3). Among these configurations, the cell with N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane exhibited superior performance in both charge and discharge capacities, indicating an optimal balance. This configuration also showed the lowest permeability to vanadium ions and minimized self-discharge, which are key factors in enhancing overall cell efficiency. In contrast, the cell with N-CQDs 1.6 composite membrane displayed poorer performance than even the N-recast membrane, which can likely be attributed to an uneven distribution of CQDs within the composite membrane, particularly due to the higher concentration of CQDs in this variant (Discussion section 1.1 and Fig. S2). This uneven distribution may hinder consistent ion transport and contribute to less effective cell operation. As a result, the VRFB single cell employing the N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane emerged as the most efficient in terms of Coulombic efficiency (CE), outperforming those with N-recast, N-CQDs 0.8, and N-CQDs 1.6 membranes. This highlights the importance of optimizing CQD concentration within the membrane to achieve the best possible performance.

In VRFB single cell configurations, excessive migration of vanadium ions through the Ion Exchange Membrane (IEM) can lead to pronounced self-discharge, resulting in a swift decline in the cell's voltage. To ascertain the magnitude of this self-discharge, the open circuit voltage (OCV) decay of the single cell was gauged when it was fully charged, i.e., at 100 % state of charge (SOC). Fig. 7b illustrates the OCV decay curves for VRFB single cells using different membrane configurations. Notably, the cell incorporating N-CQDs 1.6 showed prolonged OCV retention, maintaining voltage above 0.8 V for approximately 169.2 h. This significantly exceeds the durations observed with N-recast, N-CQDs 0.8, and N-CQDs 1.2 membranes, which recorded OCV durations of 113.8 h, 121.0 h, and 132.4 h, respectively. This enhancement in OCV duration indicates that CQDs within the membrane effectively limit the migration of vanadium ions, aligning with prior observations on the permeability characteristics of these composites. Further insights are provided in Fig. 7c, which details the performance efficiencies—coulombic efficiency (CE), voltage efficiency (VE), and energy efficiency (EE)—of cells using N-recast and N-CQDs 1.2 composite membranes. As current density increased from 40 to 120 mA cm-2, a noticeable improvement in CE was observed. The N-CQDs 1.2 cell exhibited consistent CE, even with increased current density, attributed to its enhanced ability to minimize vanadium ion crossover. This reduction in self-discharge supports higher CE values. At 40 mA cm-2, the EE for the cell using N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane reached approximately 88.2 %. Despite a decline at higher currents due to increased over-potential and ohmic resistance, the EE remained impressively high at about 81.9 % even at 120 mA/cm2, underscoring the cell's robust proton transport during intense charge-discharge cycles. Table S2 provides a comparative analysis, showing that the N-CQDs 1.2 cell surpasses other modified Nafion membrane VRFB cells in energy efficiency at high current densities (120 mA cm-2). These findings underscore the potential of N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane in enhancing the operational stability and efficiency of VRFB systems.

Fig. 8 displays the discharge capacity retention for VRFB single cells across multiple cycles, offering a critical assessment of vanadium ion permeability during operation. Remarkably, the cell equipped with N-CQDs 1.2 exhibits superior cycle stability, consistently maintaining its discharge capacity through 100 cycles. In sharp contrast, cells containing N-recast, N-CQDs 0.8, and N-CQDs 1.6 composite membranes experienced significant capacity reductions after just 27 cycles. Further analysis presented in Fig. 9 and detailed in Table S2 compares the capacity decay rates across various Nafion-based composite membranes. Notably, the VRFB cell utilizing the N-CQDs 1.2 membrane not only persevered for 100 cycles but also demonstrated a relatively low-capacity decay rate, averaging only about 0.171 % per cycle. This performance underscores the enhanced efficiency and durability imparted by the N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane, distinguishing it significantly from other configurations in prolonged operational conditions.

In conclusion, our development of sustainable Nafion/CQDs composite membranes represents a significant advancement for vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFBs). By integrating carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are rich in hydroxyl groups, into the Nafion matrix, we have successfully enhanced proton transport pathways and reduced vanadium ion permeability. Among the variants tested, the N-CQDs 1.2 composite membrane stands out, demonstrating superior ion selectivity, operational efficiency, and longevity, particularly under high current densities. Additionally, it maintains robust chemical stability, making it highly suitable for long-term applications. CQDs derived from human hair are not only environmentally friendly but also potent as components in composite membranes for VRFB applications. The N-CQDs composite membrane proves to be a formidable material that significantly boosts the performance of Nafion-based membranes in large-scale energy storage solutions. Indeed, the introduction of biomass-derived fillers into Nafion composites is a compelling and effective strategy for enhancing the capabilities of VRFB energy storage devices. This approach holds the promise of advancing sustainable energy technologies and underscores our commitment to developing eco-friendly energy solutions.

Dasom Jeong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Seong Cheon Kim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Min Gu Kang: Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sung Nam Lim: Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ju Young Woo: Methodology, Data curation. Haejin Hwang: Supervision. Siyoung Q. Choi: Supervision. Jeasung Park: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
